title: Advanced Data Structures
summary: Tries, segment trees, Union-Find, monotonic stacks, and heap tricks for competitive programming and system design.
guide:
# Advanced Data Structures

## Why Go Beyond Arrays and HashMaps?

Standard data structures handle 80% of problems. The remaining 20% — problems that
require range queries, prefix operations, union/find relationships, or priority access
— have specialised structures that are asymptotically faster.

Think of it as having more tools in your toolkit. A hammer solves most things,
but a screwdriver is fundamentally better for screws.

---

## Trie (Prefix Tree)

```
A Trie stores strings by breaking them into characters.
Each node = one character. Prefix sharing = shared nodes.

Insert: ['cat', 'car', 'card', 'dog']

        root
        /  \
       c    d
       |    |
       a    o
      / \   |
     t   r  g*
     *   |
         d*
         |
         (also 'car'*)

* = end of word

Operations: O(L) where L = string length (independent of number of strings!)
  Insert 'car':   follow c→a→r, mark r as endOfWord
  Search 'car':   follow c→a→r, check endOfWord = true
  StartsWith 'ca': follow c→a, exists? = true
  All words with prefix 'ca': DFS from 'a' node → [cat, car, card]

When to use Trie:
  - Autocomplete (type 'ca' → suggest 'cat', 'car', 'card')
  - Spell check (is this a valid word?)
  - IP routing (longest prefix match for network routes)
  - Word search in a grid (LeetCode Word Search II)
```

```java
class TrieNode {
    TrieNode[] children = new TrieNode[26]; // a-z
    boolean isEnd = false;
}

class Trie {
    private TrieNode root = new TrieNode();

    public void insert(String word) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : word.toCharArray()) {
            int i = c - 'a';
            if (node.children[i] == null)
                node.children[i] = new TrieNode();
            node = node.children[i];
        }
        node.isEnd = true;
    }

    public boolean search(String word) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : word.toCharArray()) {
            int i = c - 'a';
            if (node.children[i] == null) return false;
            node = node.children[i];
        }
        return node.isEnd;
    }
}
```

---

## Segment Tree

```
PROBLEM: Array [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
  Query: sum from index 2 to 5?      O(n) naive
  Update: change index 3 to 7?       O(1) naive
  But: 100K queries + 100K updates?  O(n) per query = too slow

SEGMENT TREE: O(log n) for both query and update

Array:  [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
 idx:   [0  1  2  3  4  5  6  7]

                [0-7: 31]
               /            \
        [0-3: 9]            [4-7: 22]
        /      \            /       \
   [0-1: 4]  [2-3: 5]  [4-5: 14]  [6-7: 8]
    /   \    /    \     /    \     /    \
   [3]  [1] [4]  [1]  [5]  [9]  [2]  [6]

Query sum [2..5]:
  Decompose into: [2-3: 5] + [4-5: 14] = 19  (2 node lookups, not 4)

Update index 3 = 7:
  Update leaf [1→7], propagate up:
  [2-3: 5→11], [0-3: 9→15], [0-7: 31→37]
  (touch only log n nodes)
```

```java
class SegmentTree {
    int[] tree;
    int n;

    SegmentTree(int[] arr) {
        n = arr.length;
        tree = new int[4 * n];
        build(arr, 0, 0, n - 1);
    }

    void build(int[] arr, int node, int start, int end) {
        if (start == end) { tree[node] = arr[start]; return; }
        int mid = (start + end) / 2;
        build(arr, 2*node+1, start, mid);
        build(arr, 2*node+2, mid+1, end);
        tree[node] = tree[2*node+1] + tree[2*node+2];
    }

    int query(int node, int start, int end, int l, int r) {
        if (r < start || end < l) return 0;  // no overlap
        if (l <= start && end <= r) return tree[node];  // total overlap
        int mid = (start + end) / 2;
        return query(2*node+1, start, mid, l, r)
             + query(2*node+2, mid+1, end, l, r);
    }
}
```

---

## Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union — DSU)

```
PROBLEM: Given a graph with edges added one at a time,
  'Are nodes A and B in the same connected component?'
  'Does adding this edge create a cycle?'

Union-Find maintains groups (sets) of elements.
Two operations: find(x) = which group is x in? union(x, y) = merge groups.

Visual:
  Initial: {1} {2} {3} {4} {5}    (each node is its own group)

  union(1, 2): {1,2} {3} {4} {5}
  union(2, 3): {1,2,3} {4} {5}
  union(4, 5): {1,2,3} {4,5}

  find(1) == find(3)?  YES (same component)
  find(1) == find(4)?  NO  (different components)

OPTIMISATIONS (make both operations nearly O(1)):
  1. Union by rank: attach smaller tree under larger tree
  2. Path compression: find() flattens tree as it traverses

  Path compression:
    Before: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 (root)
    find(1) traverses to root (4)
    After: 1 → 4, 2 → 4, 3 → 4  (all point directly to root)
    Next find(1): O(1) — directly to root

Time: O(α(n)) per operation — inverse Ackermann, effectively O(1)
```

```java
class UnionFind {
    int[] parent, rank;

    UnionFind(int n) {
        parent = new int[n];
        rank = new int[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = i;
    }

    int find(int x) {
        if (parent[x] != x)
            parent[x] = find(parent[x]);  // path compression
        return parent[x];
    }

    boolean union(int x, int y) {
        int px = find(x), py = find(y);
        if (px == py) return false;  // already same component (cycle!)
        if (rank[px] < rank[py]) { int t = px; px = py; py = t; }
        parent[py] = px;
        if (rank[px] == rank[py]) rank[px]++;
        return true;
    }
}
```

---

## Heap Tricks Beyond Priority Queue

```
Top-K pattern (k largest elements from stream of N):

  Naive: sort everything → O(N log N)
  Heap:  maintain min-heap of size k → O(N log k)

  Algorithm:
    For each number x in stream:
      If heap.size() < k: add x
      Else if x > heap.peek(): heap.poll(), heap.add(x)
    Result: heap contains k largest elements

  Why min-heap for k LARGEST?
    heap.peek() = current smallest of top-k.
    New element > smallest of top-k → it belongs in top-k → evict smallest.

  Java:
    PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();  // min-heap by default
    PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.reverseOrder());

Median from data stream:
  Two heaps: maxHeap (lower half) + minHeap (upper half)
  Invariant: maxHeap.size() == minHeap.size() OR maxHeap.size() == minHeap.size()+1
  Median: maxHeap.peek() (odd total) or avg of both peeks (even total)
  Each insert: O(log n). Query median: O(1).
```

---

## Monotonic Stack

```
A stack that maintains elements in monotonically increasing or decreasing order.
Used for 'Next Greater Element', histogram area, temperature problems.

Next Greater Element: for each element, find the next element to its right that is larger.

arr = [2, 5, 3, 7, 1]
      [5, 7, 7,-1,-1]  ← next greater, -1 if none

Algorithm (monotonic decreasing stack):
  For each element x:
    While stack not empty AND stack.top() < x:
      result[stack.pop()] = x  ← x is the next greater for popped element
    stack.push(index of x)

  [2]: push 2. stack=[2]
  [5]: 5 > 2, pop 2, result[0]=5. push 5. stack=[5]
  [3]: 3 < 5, push. stack=[5,3]
  [7]: 7 > 3, result[2]=7. 7 > 5, result[1]=7. push 7. stack=[7]
  [1]: 1 < 7, push. stack=[7,1]
  End: remaining in stack get result -1

Time: O(n) — each element pushed and popped at most once.
Use for: stock span, largest rectangle in histogram, trapping rain water.
```

---

## Mind Map

```
ADVANCED DATA STRUCTURES
|
+-- TRIE
|   +-- Prefix tree: O(L) operations
|   +-- Use: autocomplete, spell check, IP routing
|   +-- children[26] array at each node
|
+-- SEGMENT TREE
|   +-- Range query + point update: O(log n)
|   +-- Build: O(n), 4n array size
|   +-- Use: range sum, range min/max
|
+-- UNION-FIND
|   +-- Connected components, cycle detection
|   +-- Path compression + union by rank = O(α(n))
|   +-- Use: MST (Kruskal), number of islands
|
+-- HEAP TRICKS
|   +-- Top-k: min-heap of size k
|   +-- Median stream: maxHeap + minHeap split
|
+-- MONOTONIC STACK
    +-- Maintain sorted invariant
    +-- O(n) for next greater/smaller
    +-- Use: histogram, stock span, rain water
```

---

## References

### Videos
- **Advanced Data Structures** by William Fiset (Google engineer):
  https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLDV1Zeh2NRsB6SWUrDFW2RmDotAfPbeHu
- **Segment Trees** by Errichto (competitive programmer):
  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rYBtViWXYeI

### Practice
- **LeetCode** — search 'segment tree', 'trie', 'union find' tags
- **CSES Problem Set**: https://cses.fi/problemset/ — best structured competitive programming problems

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity for inserting a word of length L into a Trie?
choices: O(n) where n = number of words
questions.explanation: Insertion follows the string character by character, creating nodes as needed. The number of operations = length of the word = L. The number of other words in the trie has no effect.

questions.prompt: A Trie is used to find all words with prefix 'ca'. What algorithm traverses all matching words after reaching the 'a' node?
choices: BFS or DFS from the 'a' node collecting all paths that reach an endOfWord node
questions.explanation: After following 'c'→'a', we do a DFS from the 'a' node, building the word as we recurse. Every time we reach a node with isEnd=true, we record the current word. This is the autocomplete algorithm.

questions.prompt: Segment tree with 8 elements. A range query [2..5] processes how many nodes maximum?
choices: O(log n) = ~4 nodes — segment tree splits query into at most 4 non-overlapping segments
questions.explanation: Segment tree query: any range can be covered by at most O(4 log n) nodes because at each level, at most 4 node boundaries are crossed. In practice, range [2..5] on 8 elements requires 2-3 nodes.

questions.prompt: In Union-Find, what does path compression do?
choices: After find(x) traverses to the root, it makes all visited nodes point directly to the root — future finds on these nodes are O(1)
questions.explanation: Path compression flattens the tree lazily. During find(), as we walk up to root, we update each visited node's parent to point directly to root. The tree flattens over time, making amortised operations nearly O(1).

questions.prompt: You're detecting cycles in an undirected graph using Union-Find. Edge (u,v) is added. What indicates a cycle?
choices: find(u) == find(v) — both nodes already in same component, so adding this edge creates a cycle
questions.explanation: If find(u) == find(v) before calling union(), u and v are already in the same connected component. Adding an edge between them creates a cycle. union() returns false in this case.

questions.prompt: Why use a min-heap (not max-heap) for finding the k LARGEST elements?
choices: Min-heap of size k: peek() = smallest of the k-largest so far. New element > peek? Replace it. At end, heap contains k largest. O(n log k) vs O(n log n) sort.
questions.explanation: Counter-intuitive but correct. Keep a min-heap of the k largest seen so far. The root is the smallest of those k. When a new element is larger than the root, it evicts the smallest k-large (root) and joins. Final heap = k largest.

questions.prompt: Finding the median of a stream of numbers using two heaps. What is the invariant?
choices: maxHeap (lower half) + minHeap (upper half). maxHeap.size() == minHeap.size() OR one more. Median = maxHeap.peek() or average of both tops.
questions.explanation: Split stream in half: lower half in maxHeap, upper half in minHeap. Maintain: maxHeap.peek() <= minHeap.peek() (max of lower <= min of upper). Median: if sizes equal, average both tops; if odd, top of larger heap.

questions.prompt: Monotonic stack: what property makes it different from a regular stack?
choices: Elements are maintained in sorted order (increasing or decreasing) — elements that violate the order are popped before pushing, making previous answers available in O(1)
questions.explanation: A monotonic stack pops elements that would violate the sorted invariant before pushing new elements. The popping step is where the answer is computed (e.g., 'x is the next greater element for all popped elements').

questions.prompt: Trie vs HashMap for storing 1M strings to support prefix queries: which is better?
choices: Trie: prefix queries are O(L), impossible with HashMap. HashMap can check exact strings O(L average) but cannot enumerate all strings with prefix 'ab' efficiently.
questions.explanation: HashMap stores whole strings. Prefix query 'find all strings starting with ab' requires scanning all 1M strings. Trie: follow 'a'→'b', then DFS to collect all children. Trie is the only data structure with native O(L) prefix operations.

questions.prompt: Segment tree: why is the array size 4*n?
choices: For n leaves, a complete binary tree needs at most 4n nodes to safely accommodate all n leaf nodes and their parents at all levels — prevents array index out of bounds
questions.explanation: A perfect binary tree with n leaves has 2n-1 nodes. But Nth element at depth d might be at index 2^(d+1)-1 in the array. For safety, 4n is used to avoid index overflow for non-power-of-2 input sizes.

questions.prompt: Union-Find: union by rank means what?
choices: Attach the tree with smaller rank (height upper bound) under the tree with larger rank — keeps tree height bounded at O(log n)
questions.explanation: Without union by rank, repeated unions could create a chain: 1→2→3→...→n where find(1) takes O(n). By always making smaller tree a subtree of larger tree, height stays at O(log n). Combined with path compression: O(α(n)) amortised.

questions.prompt: What Kruskal's MST algorithm property makes Union-Find essential?
choices: Kruskal adds edges in weight order, skipping edges where both endpoints are already connected (would form cycle). Union-Find checks 'same component?' in O(α(n)) — the critical inner loop operation.
questions.explanation: Kruskal's: sort edges by weight, add edge if it connects two different components. 'Different components?' = find(u) != find(v). Then union(u,v) to merge them. Without Union-Find, cycle detection would need BFS/DFS per edge = O(E*(V+E)).

questions.prompt: After inserting words ['cat','car','card'] into a Trie, how many TrieNode objects exist total?
choices: 6 — c,a are shared. Cat: t. Car: r. Card: r,d. Root shared. Total: root+c+a+t+r+d = 6 unique nodes
questions.explanation: cat: root→c→a→t (4 nodes). car: shares root,c,a — adds r (1 node). card: shares root,c,a,r — adds d (1 node). Total: 4+1+1=6 nodes. Trie's space efficiency comes from shared prefixes.

questions.prompt: Segment tree update: changing index 3 in an 8-element array. How many nodes are updated?
choices: O(log n) = 4 nodes — only the leaf and its ancestors on the path to root
questions.explanation: Update propagates up from leaf to root. Log2(8) = 3 levels above leaf = 4 nodes total. Each ancestor's sum is recomputed. This is why segment trees are efficient: point update touches only O(log n) nodes.

questions.prompt: In the 'Next Greater Element' monotonic stack problem, what does it mean when we pop an element?
choices: The current new element is the answer (next greater) for the popped element — we record this before popping
questions.explanation: We maintain a decreasing stack. When new element x is larger than top, we pop: the popped element's 'next greater' is x. We keep popping until stack is empty or top >= x. This is the key insight: popping = answering.

questions.prompt: Largest Rectangle in Histogram: why does a monotonic stack solve it in O(n)?
choices: For each bar, we need left and right boundaries where bars are shorter. Monotonic stack tracks these boundaries — when we pop a bar, the current bar is its right boundary, stack top is its left boundary. O(1) per element.
questions.explanation: Classic monotonic stack application. Left boundary of bar i = previous shorter bar's index + 1 = stack top after popping. Right boundary = current shorter bar = trigger of the pop. Area = height[i] × (right-left-1). Each bar pushed/popped once = O(n).

questions.prompt: What does 'Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree)' offer over Segment Tree?
choices: Simpler implementation (array + single BIT operation), same O(log n) for point update and prefix sum query, half the memory of segment tree. Trade-off: less flexible (prefix sums only, harder to extend to range min/max).
questions.explanation: BIT is simpler to code (10 lines vs 50+). Same asymptotic complexity for prefix sum queries. Half memory. But segment tree is more general: supports range queries for any associative operation, range updates with lazy propagation.

questions.prompt: Union-Find with N=10^6 nodes, performing 10^6 union and find operations. Practical time complexity?
choices: O(N × α(N)) ≈ O(N) — α(N) is the inverse Ackermann function, never exceeds 5 for any practical N
questions.explanation: α(N) (inverse Ackermann) grows so slowly it's effectively constant. For N = 10^80 (atoms in observable universe), α(N) = 5. For practical purposes: Union-Find with both optimisations is O(1) per operation.

questions.prompt: You need to find the k-th smallest element in a matrix where each row and column is sorted. Best approach?
choices: Sort all elements O(n² log n)
Min-heap of (value, row, col) starting with first column elements. Poll k times, push next element in same row each time. O(k log n) where n is matrix dimension.
questions.explanation: Each matrix row is sorted. Min-heap starts with (matrix[i][0], i, 0) for all rows. Poll min: if col+1 exists, push (matrix[i][col+1], i, col+1). After k polls = k-th smallest. Uses sorted structure to avoid processing all n² elements.

questions.prompt: What is the space complexity of a Trie storing N words of average length L?
choices: O(N × L) worst case (no shared prefixes)
questions.explanation: Worst case: all words share no prefixes (completely different first characters). Each word needs L nodes. N words × L nodes = O(NL). Best case (all words share most of the path): much better. Dictionary words often share prefixes, giving good practical compression.

flashcards.front: When to use Trie over HashMap
flashcards.back: Use Trie when you need prefix operations: autocomplete, 'all words starting with X', longest prefix match. HashMap is O(1) but can't enumerate by prefix. Trie: O(L) per operation, L = word length.

Both O(log n). Build: O(n). Space: 4n array. Use for range sum/min/max with point updates. When range is exact node: return directly. When no overlap: return 0/INF. When partial: recurse both children.
find(x): returns root of x's group (with path compression). union(x,y): merge groups of x and y (by rank). Returns false if already same group (= cycle). O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) with both optimisations.

k largest: maintain min-heap of size k. For each element: if heap.size() < k OR element > heap.peek(): replace. O(n log k). k smallest: max-heap same logic. Java: PriorityQueue (min) or new PriorityQueue(Comparator.reverseOrder()) (max).

Two heaps: maxHeap (lower half) + minHeap (upper half). Balance: sizes differ by at most 1. maxHeap.peek() <= minHeap.peek(). Median: even size = average of both tops. Odd = top of larger heap. O(log n) insert, O(1) query.

Use when you need 'next/previous greater/smaller element'. Decreasing stack for next greater. Increasing stack for next smaller. O(n) total — each element pushed and popped at most once. Problems: stock span, histogram, rain water.

Before union(u,v): if find(u) == find(v) → cycle! They're already connected. Used in Kruskal's MST: skip edge if endpoints are in same component. Also: number of connected components = number of distinct roots.

Segment Tree: general (any associative op: sum, min, max), range updates, lazy propagation. Fenwick Tree: prefix sums only, simpler code, half memory. For competitive programming: learn both. For interviews: know segment tree.

project.brief: Design a real-time leaderboard for a game with 1 million players. Features: update a player's score, get player's rank, get top-100 players, get players in rank range [50..100]. Design the data structure and algorithm for each operation. What are the time and space complexities?

checklist.text: Score update: O(log n) using balanced BST or segment tree indexed by score range
Top-100 query: O(100 log n) with heap or O(log n + 100) with segment tree
Player rank lookup: O(log n) with order-statistic tree or segment tree with counts
Redis sorted set real-world option: ZADD O(log n), ZREVRANK O(log n), ZREVRANGE O(log n + k)
Space trade-offs discussed: segment tree on score range vs tree on player array

hints: Redis Sorted Set is the real-world answer: ZADD gameLeaderboard <score> <playerId>. ZREVRANK for rank. ZRANGE for range.
Segment tree: build over score range [0..10^9] with coordinate compression. Each leaf = count of players at that score.
Order-statistic tree (Java TreeMap won't work natively — you'd need a custom AVL/Red-Black that tracks subtree sizes).
